coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from sodium citrate and polyacrylamide and their highly selective detection of lead and pyrophosphate
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yongli
Zhou, Qingxiang
Yuan, Yongyong
Wu, Yalin
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been a promising star in analytical and environmental fields. Present study designed a new bright-blue fluorescent carbon dots with sodium citrate and polyacrylamide by a hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs had an average diameter of 2.4 nm and exhibited excitation-independent property. The experimental results demonstrated that the bright-blue fluorescent CDs exhibited “off-on” property with Pb2+ and pyrophosphate (PPi), which proved that it was a good fluorescent probe for the determination of Pb2+ and PPi. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was significantly quenched by Pb2+ (turn-off) through forming CDs/Pb2+ complexes via an inner filter effect, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs/Pb2+ system was completely resumed by PPi (turn-on) owing to the release of CDs from the CDs/Pb2+ complexes due to higher binding force of PPi to Pb2+. The detection limits were 4.6 nM for Pb2+ and 54 nM for PPi, respectively. The probe was successfully validated with real water samples and human urine. The results showed that this the probe was facile, rapid and exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, repeatability and stability. This “off-on” fluorescent probe based on CDs provided a promising platform for environmental and biological sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Pb2+
Pyrophosphate

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence detection for Pb2+ and PPi
Fluorescence responses of CDs and the mechanism for the detection of Pb2+ and PPi
Precisions and stability for detection of Pb2+ and PPi

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation for CDs and their application for Pb2+ and PPi determination. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) TEM and (B) HRTEM images of CDs (the inset is the size distribution histogram of CDs). (C) XRD pattern of CDs. (D) Raman spectrum of CDs. (E) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (F) High resolution XPS spectrum of C1s. (G) High resolution XPS spectrum of O1s. (H) High resolution XPS spectrum of N1s. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) UV–vis absorption (a) and FL (excitation spectrum: b, emission spectrum: c) spectra of the CDs. (B) FL spectra of the CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths. (C) The contour plot (3D plot) of CDs. (D) Effect of the solution pH on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. (E) Effect of the concentration of NaCl on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. (F) Stability of CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The fluorescence spectra of CDs under different conditions. (B) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs under different conditions. (C) Zeta potentials of the CDs, CDs with Pb2+ and CDs with Pb2+ and PPi under the same condition. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A)The contour plot of CDs and CDs-Pb2+. (B) The optimization of CDs concentration. (C) The optimization of pH value. (D) The optimization of incubation time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The fluorescence response of the CDs upon the addition of different concentrations of Pb2+. (B) Plot of the fluorescence intensity against Pb2+ concentration within the range of 0–4.0 μM. Inset: a linear correlation of (F0-F)/F0 values versus the concentration of Pb2+ over the range from 0.0167 to 1.0 μM. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Selectivity of CDs for Pb2+ over other metal ions (black bars) and the mixture of Pb2+ with other metal ions (red bars). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The contour plot of CDs/Pb2+-PPi. (B) The optimization of CDs concentration. (C) The optimization of incubation time. (D) The optimization of pH value. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The fluorescence response of the CDs-Pb2+ system with the addition of different concentrations of PPi. (B) Plot of the fluorescence intensity against PPi concentration within the range of 0–100 μM. Inset: a linear correlation of (F2- F1) values versus the concentration of PPi over the range from 0.67 to 26.7 μM. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Selectivity of CDs/Pb2+ system for PPi over other anions (blue bars) and the mixture of PPi with other anions (red bars). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The stability of the method for Pb2+ and PPi detection. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Spiked recoveries for Pb2+ from real water samples.
Spiked recoveries of PPi in human urine.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from sodium citrate and polyacrylamide and their highly selective detection of lead and pyrophosphate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Geosciences, China University of Petroleum, Beijing, 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been a promising star in analytical and environmental fields. Present study designed a new bright-blue fluorescent carbon dots with sodium citrate and polyacrylamide by a hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs had an average diameter of 2.4 nm and exhibited excitation-independent property. The experimental results demonstrated that the bright-blue fluorescent CDs exhibited “off-on” property with Pb2+ and pyrophosphate (PPi), which proved that it was a good fluorescent probe for the determination of Pb2+ and PPi. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was significantly quenched by Pb2+ (turn-off) through forming CDs/Pb2+ complexes via an inner filter effect, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs/Pb2+ system was completely resumed by PPi (turn-on) owing to the release of CDs from the CDs/Pb2+ complexes due to higher binding force of PPi to Pb2+. The detection limits were 4.6 nM for Pb2+ and 54 nM for PPi, respectively. The probe was successfully validated with real water samples and human urine. The results showed that this the probe was facile, rapid and exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, repeatability and stability. This “off-on” fluorescent probe based on CDs provided a promising platform for environmental and biological sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Contamination of heavy metal ions has attracted serious concern due to their highly toxic and bio-accumulative properties [1,2]. Lead ion (Pb2+), as a kind of common and widely used heavy metal ions, impacts the ecological environment and health of human even at a very low concentration. If the concentration of Pb2+ is more than 5 μM in blood, it will cause anemia, reproductive dysfunction, developmental disorders and nervous system dysfunction [3,4]. So far, a number of instrumental techniques, such as electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (IES), atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) and inductive coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), have been widely applied for the determination of Pb2+ [5–7]. However these methods usually need sophisticated operation, high costs and long analysis time. On the other hand, pyrophosphate (P2O4 7− or PPi) is released upon hydrolysis of adenosine triphosphate to adenosine monophosphate under cellular conditions and it is also an inhibitor of several crystallization reactions. Detection and quantification of PPi have an important significance to help identifying diseases such as chondrocalcinosis or calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate (CPPD) crystal deposition disease [8–10]. Up to date, most of determination methods for PPi are on the base of organic small-molecule probes, which are complicated and time-consuming [11]. Thus, it is necessary to develop some relatively inexpensive, rapid, sensitive and selective methods for detecting Pb2+ and PPi at trace level.

As a new class of carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) were firstly discovered in the purification procedure of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [12], which have absorbed enormous attention from the researchers. CDs have spherical structure with the size below 10 nm and display strong and stable fluorescence. Compared to other fluorescent materials such as organic dye molecules, metal nanoparticles and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have a number of remarkable properties such as easy preparation, excellent aqueous solubility, fluorescent stability, low toxicity, outstanding biocompatibility and environmental friendliness, which make them widely used in sensing [13], bioimaging [14], photocatalysis [15], optoelectronic devices [16], and drug delivery [17]. Recently, many methods have been developed for the preparation of fluorescent CDs, such as arc discharge [18], laser ablation [19], electrochemical oxidation [20], acidic oxidation [21,22], combustion [23], hydrothermal treatment [24], ultrasonic treatment [25] and microwave-assisted methods [26], etc. Currently, many CDs have been developed as fluorescent probes for the determination of metal ions based on the fluorescence change in aqueous solutions. Gao et al. established a facile and green method to synthesize amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) with anhydrous citric acid as carbon precursors, sodium borohydride as reducing agent and ammonia as the passivation agent, which exhibited a good sensitivity with the detection limit of 20 nM for the determination of Hg2+ in water samples [27]. Gedda et al. prepared a kind of CDs from prawn shells, which was used as an excellently selective and sensitive sensor for the determination of Cu2+ with a low detection limit of 5 nM [28]. Jiang and co-workers synthesized high fluorescence Silicon-Carbon-Based Dots@Dopamine (Si-CDs@DA) for highly sensitive detection of Ag+ on the basis of fluorescence quenching mechanism, and the detection limit decreased to 2.5 nM [29]. As lead ion was concerned, a fluorescent probe based on CDs derived from chocolate was prepared and which exhibited highly sensitivity and selectivity for Pb2+, and the detection limit was 12.7 nM [30].

The goal of present study is to carry out an attempt to prepare a new CD with sodium citrate and polyacrylamide, and further develop an effective fluorescent probe for highly sensitive determination of Pb2+ and PPi. Pb2+ can chelate with the prepared CDs to form the CDs/Pb2+ complexes, due to the strong interaction between Pb2+ and the specific groups on the surface of CDs, which will lead to the fluorescence quenching, as termed “turn-off” effect. The fluorescence of CDs/Pb2+ system will be restored by the addition of PPi due to the stronger binding preference between Pb2+ and PPi, as termed “turn-on” effect. Hence the CDs was used to design an “off-on” fluorescence probe for highly sensitive and selective detection of Pb2+ and PPi. The determination process was demonstrated in Fig. 1 . Besides, some critical parameters related to the detection such as the concentration of CDs, the incubation time, pH of CDs solutions had been investigated and optimized to obtain good sensing performance for the determination of Pb2+ and PPi.

Acrylamide, quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for fluorescence), triethylamine hydrochloride, ammonium molybdate were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium citrate was purchased from J&K Scientific Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Multi-walled carbon nanotubes were purchased from Shenzhen Nanotech Port (Shenzhen, China). All other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China) without further purification. Purified water was used throughout all the experiments.

Before synthesis of CDs, the polyacrylamide was prepared. In the procedure, 5.0 g acrylamide and 80 mL purified water were added into a 250 mL three flask, and heated for 15 min at 30 °C under stirring. Further, 0.025 g potassium persulfate dissolved in 10 mL purified water was added, and heated at 90 °C for 2 h with stirring. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was concentrated by rotary evaporation and the product was dried in an oven for 2 h. For the synthesis of CDs, 1.0 g sodium citrate and 0.52 g polyacrylamide were dissolved in 20 mL purified water. After vigorous stirring, the mixture was added into a 50 mL PTFE reaction kettle and reacted in an electroheat baking oven at 200 °C for 3 h. After the kettle was cooled to ambient temperature naturally, a blue-green solution was obtained. And then, the solution containing the CDs was collected after dialyzing against purified water for 3 h and dried in an oven. Finally, the CDs were dispersed in purified water at a concentration of 1.0 mg mL−1 for subsequent experiments.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a TU-1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PGeneral, Beijing, China). Fluorescence measurements were measured on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The morphologies were achieved by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Akishima-shi, Japan) using an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were carried out on a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, New York, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed on a Thermo Fisher K-Alpha spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, New York, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was obtained on a Philips D/Max-2500 multipurpose X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation at 40 kV and 200 mA. (Philips, Amsterdam, Holland). Raman spectra were measured with an inVia Raman microscope with a laser at an excitation wavelength of 633 nm (Renishaw, London, England). Zeta potential measurements were performed on a Zetasizer Nano ZS zeta potential analyzer (Malvern, Malvern, England).
Relative fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs was determined using quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (literature QY: 0.54 at 340 nm) as a reference by the following equation [31],

Here the subscripts “s” and “r” denote sample and reference, respectively. Q refers to Quantum yield. I is the integrated emission intensity (Excitation wavelength, 360 nm). A represents the UV–vis absorbance which is kept below 0.05 to minimize reabsorption effects, and n is the refractive index with 1.33 as the default for both quinine sulfate and CDs solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection for Pb2+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Pb2+, 15 μL of CDs solution and different amounts of Pb2+ were added into a 2.0 mL centrifuge tube, and the mixture was diluted to 1.5 mL with purified water and mixed completely. Twenty metal cations such as Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, As3+, As5+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+ were chosen to assess the influence of co-existing ions and evaluate the selectivity for Pb2+.

For the detection of PPi, 15 μL of CDs solution, 15 μL of Pb2+ (0.1 mM) and then different amounts of PPi were added into a 2.0 mL centrifuge tube, and then the mixture was diluted to 1.5 mL with purified water and mixed completely. Sixteen anions such as F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, S2−, BrO3 −, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, S2O3 2−, S2O8 2−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 −, NO3 − and SCN− were chosen to assess the influence of co-existing ions on the fluorescence of CDs/Pb2+ system and evaluate the selectivity for PPi. The fluorescence spectra were collected at room temperature subsequently from 353 nm to 580 nm with excitation wavelength at 343 nm, and the slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 5 nm.

For the real water samples, 50 μL 1 mM barium nitrate solution was added to 50 mL the real water samples to eliminate the interference of some anions and the water samples were treated by filtering through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The blank and spiked samples with different concentration levels of Pb2+ were analyzed.

For the determination of PPi in the urine sample, 100 mL urine sample was mixed with 0.1 mL 6 M hydrochloric acid, and then the mixture was purified using 0.2 g multi-walled carbon nanotubes. Next, 1.6 mL 1 M perchloric acid and 0.4 mL 0.08 M ammonium molybdate were added into 7.0 mL decolorized sample, and then 0.8 mL 0.1 M triethylamine hydrochloride (pH 5.0) was added after mixing thoroughly. The mixture was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min, and the clear supernatant solution was centrifuged again for 20 min at 10000 rpm. Some quantity of a suitable concentration of sodium hydroxide was added into the urine sample to precipitate all of the Mg2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+. The pH of the clear supernatant was adjusted to pH7.0 with hydrochloric acid after centrifugation for 15 min at 4000 rpm. Then, 15 μL 1.0 mg mL−1 of CDs stock solutions, 15 μL 0.1 mM of Pb2+ solutions, and 15 μL of the treated urine sample were mixed and diluted to 1.5 mL with purified water. The sample was analyzed directly and then spiked samples were also analyzed.

Fig. 2 A shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the prepared CDs, which indicated that the CDs were mono-dispersed and uniform with spherical structures. However, HRTEM images of the CDs revealed that any clear lattice fringes were not found (Fig. 2B), which suggested that they were amorphous. The particle size distribution of CDs was displayed in Fig. 2B (inset) by measuring 120 random nanoparticles, which indicated that the CDs had an average diameter of 2.4 nm. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2C) exhibited a broad, low-intensity diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 20.66°, further revealing the existence of amorphous carbon [32]. In the Raman spectrum (Fig. 2D), a D band at 1363 cm−1 and a G band at 1582 cm−1 were ascribed to the presence of sp3 and sp2 carbons respectively. The intensity ratio of D and G bands is 0.91, which also indicated the CDs had an amorphous carbon phase [33,34]. FTIR spectrum of the CDs was shown in Fig. 2E. The sharp peaks at about 3436 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 were assigned to the NH/OH stretching vibration and OH bending vibration, respectively. The peak at 1590 cm−1 was associated with the asymmetric stretching vibration of the carboxylate anions or the bending vibration of NH. The peaks at 1648 cm−1 and 1059 cm−1 were possibly indicative of the CO stretching vibration and CN stretching vibrations, respectively. The XPS measurements were also utilized to gain further insight into the surface functional groups of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2F, the high resolution C1s revealed three peaks at 284.3, 285.2, 288.0 eV which were attributed to CC, CN and CO species, respectively. The peaks at 530.7, 532.0, and 534.8 eV associated with CO, COH, and CO were observed from Fig. 2G. Further, the N1s spectrum shows one peak at 399.6 eV (CN) (Fig. 2H) [35]. It is evident that these data were consistent with the above FTIR analysis. The FTIR and XPS results indicated there were functional hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs which greatly improve the water solubility and stability of the obtained CDs and also help to generate energy gaps that emit light efficiently when photo-stimulated [36–38].

As illustrated in Fig. 3 A, there were two absorption peaks at 232 and 334 nm. The peak at 232 nm was ascribed to the π-π* transitions of the CC bond and the absorption peak at 334 nm was associated with the formation of excited defect surface states, which could lead to strong fluorescence [39,40]. The maximum emission intensity was obtained at 434 nm with an excitation wavelength of 343 nm. Interestingly, the fluorescence emission peak at 434 nm displayed nearly no shift as the excitation wavelength varied in the range of 280–390 nm indicating that the CDs were excitation-independent. It was considered to be related to more uniform size of the CDs, which was consistent with TEM observation [39]. Fig. 3C was a contour plot or 3D plot between excitation wavelength vs. emission wavelength and corresponding color represent emission intensity of CDs. It was quite clear from contour plot that maximum emission was at 434 nm upon 343 nm excitation. The quantum yield of CDs was measured to be 18.0% with quinine sulfate (quantum yield 54%) as reference [31], and which was significantly comparable to some of the previously reported CDs [20,28,41].

The influence of pH and ionic strength (in terms of the concentration of NaCl) on the fluorescence stability of the obtained CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity at 434 nm increased gradually with the increase of sample pH from pH 1.0 to 3.0 and then displayed a steep increase at pH 3.0–5.0 and reached a maximum at pH 7.0. When the sample pH value continued to increase from pH 8.0 to pH 13.0, the fluorescence intensity dropped slightly (the change less than 8%). Finally, the fluorescence intensity decreased obviously at pH 14.0. We can see that The CDs have the fairly high and constant fluorescence intensity at pH 6.0–8.0 which indicate that the obtained CDs would have a great potential application in cellular imaging. As given in Fig. 3E, the fluorescence intensity had a slight decrease (the change less than 6%) as the ionic strength increases even when the concentration of NaCl was as high as 2.5 M, which indicated the CDs earned excellent optical stability. In short, the results suggested that the CDs had a promising potential for sensing applications under physiological conditions [42]. Moreover, there was no observation of precipitated or floating particles even after being kept for one month, and the fluorescence intensity of CDs displayed a negligible change, which demonstrated that the prepared CDs exhibited good fluorescent stability (See Fig. 3F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of CDs and the mechanism for the detection of Pb2+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 A displays the fluorescence responses of CDs under different conditions. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was dramatically quenched with the addition of Pb2+ and the fluorescence intensity decreased by 73% of the original intensity (Fig. 4A, curves a and b). The chelation between Pb2+ and the carboxylate, organo-nitrogen groups on the surface of CDs led to the formation of the CDs/Pb2+ complexes and further resulted in fluorescence quenching of CDs through an inner filter effect [43]. More interestingly, the quenched fluorescence intensity by Pb2+ was effectively restored in the presence of PPi, which was possibly ascribed to the stronger binding preference between Pb2+ and PPi. It was the stronger binding preference that made Pb2+ release from the CDs/Pb2+ complexes (Fig. 4A, curve c). In contrast, no detectable fluorescence was observed with the addition of PPi to the Pb2+ solution alone (Fig. 4A, curve e). Additionally, it was clear that fluorescence intensity of the CDs solution changed very little with adding of PPi alone (Fig. 4A, curve d). These results validated that the preferential binding of PPi with Pb2+ released CDs from the CDs/Pb2+ complexes which led to the restoration of fluorescence.

UV–vis spectra of this sensing system were further investigated to confirm the detection mechanism (Fig. 4B). The absorption centered at 232 nm disappeared. The strong absorption peak of CDs around 334 nm was blue-shifted to 324 nm and the absorbance was decreased distinctly in the presence of Pb2+ owing to the inner filter effect [44,45]. However, with the addition of PPi, the peak belonged to CDs was obviously restored, indicating the release of Pb2+ from the surface of CDs. Moreover, there was no observable change with the peak at 334 nm after the addition of PPi to the CDs solution alone. These results were consistent with the observations of the fluorescence spectra.

To clearly ascertain the mechanism for detection of Pb2+ and PPi, the zeta potential measurements of CDs at different conditions were also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4C, the zeta potential of CDs shifted −10.90 to −2.88 mV in the presence of Pb2+, whereas the value decreased to −9.58 mV with addition of PPi to the CDs/Pb2+ complexes solution, which indirectly supported the Pb2+ initially associated with the carboxylate, organo-nitrogen groups on the surface of CDs and then released from the CDs to bind with PPi upon its introduction [46]. All above results indicated that the CDs and CDs/Pb2+ system were able to be employed as an effective “off-on” fluorescent probe for the detection Pb2+ and PPi, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was significantly quenched in the presence of Pb2+. To obtain good sensing performances for the detection of Pb2+, some critical factors of experimental parameters such as concentration of CDs, pH of CDs solutions, and incubation time were further investigated in subsequent experiments. In the optimization steps, the concentration of Pb2+ was set at 0.67 μM. The value of (F0-F) was used as the signal for Pb2+, where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities without and with addition of Pb2+, respectively.

Generally, with the given concentration of Pb2+, the lower concentration of CDs exhibited higher sensitivity, and further higher concentration would result in a wider detection range. As demonstrated in Fig. 5B, the results showed that the concentration of CDs had significant influence on the detection of Pb2+. It was evident that the value of (F0-F) increased with the increase of the concentration of CDs and later reduced with a maximal value of (F0-F) at 10 μg mL−1. Thus, the concentration of 10 μg mL−1 was chosen for the detection of Pb2+ in the subsequent experiments. The effect of the pH of CDs solution on the (F0-F) value was shown in Fig. 5C. It revealed that the (F0-F) value increased gradually to a maximum value with the increase of pH up to pH 7.0 and later decreased. At stronger acidic conditions, some hydrogen ions in the solution could react with the carboxylate groups on the surface of CDs, leading to the decrease of the chelation between Pb2+ and CDs. When the pH was above pH 7.0, the interaction of Pb2+ with a high concentration of OH− might reduce the reaction degree of Pb2+ and CDs which would decrease the quenching effect and led to the decline of the (F0-F) value. Hence, pH 7.0 was employed as the favorable pH value. Fig. 5D exhibits the influence of the incubation time on the value of (F0-F). It was not hard to see that the fluorescence intensity of CDs was quickly quenched owing to the strong coordination ability between Pb2+ and the carboxylate and organo-nitrogen groups on the CDs surface. The (F0-F) value was stable after adding Pb2+ and changed negligibly with further increase of the incubation time, suggesting the reaction between Pb2+ and CDs was rather fast and stable. The fast formation of CDs-Pb2+ complex and their stability were highly desirable for developing reliable and robust determination for Pb2+. Thus, the CDs could be used as a fast fluorescent probe for the detection of Pb2+ without strict time control.

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe was evaluated by adding different amount of Pb2+ into the CDs solution. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at around 434 nm was gradually quenched with the addition of Pb2+ in the concentration range of 0.0167–4.0 μM under the optimal conditions. It was obvious that the fluorescence response (F0-F/F0) of CDs displayed good linearity in the concentration range from 0.0167 to 1 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.998 (Fig. 6B). The limit of detection for Pb2+ was as low as 4.6 nM based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD = 3σ/s, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 25), and s was the slope of the linear calibration curve), which was comparable to the sensitivity of some previous reports [47,48]. Moreover, the developed method had the advantages such as rapidness, broad linear range and high sensitivity.

Selectivity was another critical parameter for the detection of Pb2+ and evaluated by two series of comparative experiments. First, 0.067 μM of Pb2+ and 1.0 μM of 20 other kinds of metal ions, including Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, As3+, As5+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+ were added into the CDs solution, respectively. Then, the fluorescence responses of CDs were recorded. As shown in Fig. 7 (black bars), no distinct changes of the fluorescent quenching values (F0-F)/F0 was observed except for Pb2+. This discrimination effect may be attributed to that Pb2+ has a stronger affinity and faster chelating process with carboxylate and organo-nitrogen groups on the surface of CDs in comparison with other metal ions [49,50]. In another series of experiments, the mixtures of 0.067 μM of Pb2+ and 1.0 μM of the above-mentioned metal ions were added into the CDs solution, and then the fluorescence intensities of CDs (Fig. 7, red bars) were measured. As clearly shown by these results, the other coexisting metal ions displayed no obviously interference, which forcefully suggested that the as-prepared CDs had a high selectivity toward Pb2+ over the other biologically and environmentally relevant metal ions.

As shown in Fig. 8 A, the results suggested that the CDs-Pb2+ system could be utilized as the fluorescent probe for “turn-on” determination of PPi. Some critical factors of experimental parameters such as the concentration of CDs, the incubation time, the pH of CDs solutions were also investigated to optimize to obtain good sensing performance for PPi detection based on the fluorescence restoration of the CDs/Pb2+ system mechanism in subsequent experiments. In optimization steps, the concentration of Pb2+ was set at 1.0 μM. The (F2-F1) value was used as the signal for the determination of PPi, where F1 and F2 were the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of PPi, respectively.

As illustrated in Fig. 8B, the results showed that the concentration of CDs played an important role in the detection of PPi. The value of (F2-F1) increased as the CDs concentration increased and reached its maximal value at 10 μg mL−1. When the concentrations of CDs further increased, the value of (F2-F1) declined. Therefore, 10 μg mL−1 of CDs solution was used for further experiments. In addition, it was found that the fluorescence recovery of CDs/Pb2+ system induced by PPi was rapid and stable within 10 min (See Fig. 8C). The results indicated that the CDs/Pb2+ system could be utilized for simple and rapid determination of PPi. The influence of the pH of CDs solution on the value of (F2-F1) was exhibited in Fig. 8D. Fig. 8D shows that the value of (F2-F1) increased gradually to a maximum value with the increase of pH up to pH 7.0 and later decreased sharply. From above results, the quenching effect by Pb2+ had a maximum value at pH 7.0 which indicated that the weaker quenching effect would lead to the reduction of the value of (F2-F1) when sample pH was lower or higher than pH 7.0. Thus, the pH 7.0 was used in the following experiments.

As shown in Fig. 9 A, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/Pb2+ system was obviously recovered, and even restored to its original intensity with the increase of PPi concentration. The results indicated that the CDs/Pb2+ system was sensitive to PPi and the “off” state of CDs quenched by Pb2+ could be turned “on” by addition of PPi. As found in Fig. 9B, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs/Pb2+ system at 434 nm was gradually increased and then reached a saturation point as the PPi concentration reached 66.7 μM, continuous increase of PPi concentration did not resulted in detectable changes of the fluorescence intensity. There was a good linearity between the value of (F2-F1) and the PPi concentration in the range from 0.67 to 26.7 μM (R2 = 0.999), and the limit of detection was calculated to be 54 nM based on 3σ/s (n = 25). The sensitivity of the proposed fluorescence probe was higher than that of some previous reports [51–53], which suggested that the proposed fluorescence probe was very suitable for PPi detection.

Two series of experiments were carried out to evaluate the selectivity and specificity of CDs-Pb2+ system towards PPi. Firstly, 0.67 μM of PPi, 67 μM of F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, S2−, BrO3 −, S2O8 2−, NO3 −, SCN−, 33 μM of SO4 2−, SO3 2−, S2O3 2−, and 6.7 μM of CO3 2−, HCO3 −, H2PO4 −, HPO4 − were added into the CDs-Pb2+ solution, respectively. As shown in Fig. 10 (blue bars), the quenched fluorescence intensity was recovered immediately with the addition of PPi into the CDs/Pb2+ system, whereas no obvious signal change was observed for other anions, suggesting that high selectivity of this fluorescence probe provided a great potential for the efficient detection of PPi. Secondly, the mixtures of PPi and the above-mentioned anions were added into the CDs/Pb2+ system, respectively. It was observed that the other coexisting anions exhibited no obviously interference compared with the efficient restoration effect of PPi (Fig. 10, the red bars), which forcefully indicated that the CDs/Pb2+ system had a high selectivity toward PPi over the other relevant anions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precisions and stability for detection of Pb2+ and PPi
ce:section.ce:para: To take into account the reliability and stability of the proposed method, the intra-day, inter-day precision experiments and stability experiments were further performed. For detection of Pb2+, in the intra-day precision assays, the fluorescence responses of CDs to the concentration of Pb2+ at three levels of 0.067, 0.20 and 0.33 μM were recorded (n = 6). While the fluorescence response of CDs to the three concentrations of Pb2+ were recorded one time a day and continuously measured for six days. The experimental results showed that the intra-day precisions for 0.067, 0.20 and 0.33 μM were 3.3, 0.45, and 0.24% (RSD, n = 6), inter-day precisions for 0.067, 0.20 and 0.33 μM were 2.9, 0.49, and 0.32% (RSD, n = 6), respectively. As PPi was concerned, three concentrations of 0.67, 10 and 20 μM were utilized for obtaining the intra-day and inter-day precisions, respectively. The results showed that the intra-day precisions for 0.67, 10 and 20 μM were 1.2, 0.41, and 0.89% (RSD, n = 6), inter-day precisions for 0.67, 10 and 20 μM were 1.1, 0.39, and 0.85% (RSD, n = 6), respectively. The fluorescence response of CDs to the concentrations of Pb2+ at 0.20 μM and the fluorescence recovery of CDs/Pb2+ system to the concentrations of PPi at 10 μM were recorded and lasted for 24 h. It could be seen that the value of (F0-F)/F0 and (F2-F1) exhibited no obvious changes within 24 h (See Fig. 11 ). The experimental results confirmed that the detection system earned good stability, which ensured the reproducibility for the analysis of Pb2+ and PPi.

This probe based on CDs was used for the determination of Pb2+ and PPi in real samples (water samples and human urine). The results were exhibited in Tables 1 and 2
, respectively. It could be clearly seen that the spiked recoveries of Pb2+ were in the range of 94.5–107.5% with the RSDs in the range of 0.63–6.31%, and the spiked recoveries for PPi were in the range of 98.7–99.9% with the RSDs in the range of 0.85–2.60%, which revealed that the developed fluorescence probe had the great potential in environmental and biologic samples monitoring.

In summary, water-soluble carbon dots with strong fluorescence were prepared with sodium citrate and polyacrylamide through a convenient, facile and low-cost hydrothermal approach. The fluorescence intensity of CDs was markedly quenched by Pb2+ while the fluorescence intensity recovered significantly after the addition of PPi. Based on these phenomenon, sensitive fluorescence probes were developed for “off-on” detection of Pb2+ and PPi, which provided good sensitivity with the limit of detections of 4.6 nM and 54 nM for Pb2+ and PPi, respectively. The probes had been successfully validated with in real samples and excellent spiked recoveries were achieved. It is believed that they may offer an effective tool for the simple, rapid, low-cost, high selective and sensitive detection of Pb2+ and PPi, and which will have a great prospect in environmental and other fields in the future.
